sentences.text: I had a younger brother, Srulek, and a little sister, Zeisel.
They were also born in Ciechanow, Poland.
I-- you know, in our home, maybe because my grandmother was Orthodox, I don't know why-- we didn't celebrate, the same way, birthdays, you know?
So I don't know exactly what their birthday.
But I know that, when the war broke out, and I was 13, my little sister, Zeisel-- Zeisele, we used to call her-- she hadn't started school yet.
So she was very young.
My little brother started at three, but he never became bar mitzvah before the Holocaust.
So I know that they were younger, but I don't know how much younger.
So instead, for guessing, I rather don't say.
But I know they were the young children.
No, I was not-- I was the firstborn, but my parents used to tell me that they-- my mother had a miscarriage with the first child, and that they were so thrilled when I was born, finally.

And in fact, knowing this and telling me how welcome, I was welcome-- and something which they didn't tell me, but I took it out myself-- you know, like an assumption-- is that when my mother told me that she used to play the Yiddish theater, but she gave it up when I was born, made me feel that she-- that that was something that was the most important.
And actually it was, to her.
The family was the first important.
Assuming-- again, I don't know for sure-- every one-- my mother was one of seven siblings.
My grandmother was one of seven siblings.
And, as I mentioned before, both came from a strictly Orthodox-- both my grandparents and my great-grandparents were strictly Orthodox.
So I guess that-- and all her sisters and brothers were Orthodox.
So I assume they probably weren't too thrilled with my mother, because she was, she was already playing the Yiddish theater, which was not very much appreciated, at that time, by Hasidic Jews.
I never heard them talking about it.
This is what I'm assuming, from knowing later.
But, for me, it was a great advantage, because I was introduced early to Yiddish literature, Yiddish songs, you know, from the point of view.
And also, because my-- they had displayed a lot of affection when we were little, you know.
And Yiddish songs had a great impact on my life, and the Yiddish literature, because they were expressing the values and the times which we lived in.
For instance, there was a song that I very much remember.
And I appreciate it more now than then.
It was called the [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't remember exact all the words, but I remember the meaning of it.
And it was Reb Lazar, which means Mr.
Lazar, like, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
I'll translate it later.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Which reflected the time, most people didn't have water, so the water carriers was-- that time.
And the song-- but he was once influential, well-off-to-do Jew, and then he became poor.
So, to make a living, he carried water.
He comes home, in the first stanza, and the wife-- tired, wet, you know, from carrying-- it's hard to carry the, the pails of water.
And the wife complains she doesn't have enough vegetables for the soup.
They were poor.
So he said, foolish woman, you have bread and potatoes.
Be grateful for them.
Thank God for that.
So, the second time he comes home, the daughter complains she doesn't have a dress.
He said, foolish girl!
You have bread without potato.
Be grateful.
And the third verse, stanza, he says-- he comes home, and the wife tells him, the rabbi sent our son home from school.
I didn't have a zloty-- which means, like, a dollar-- to pay him.
And then he responds-- takes out the last zloty-- and he said, to eat-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
To eat, you need.
But to study, you have to.
Be grateful for that.
And gives away the last [? penny ?] for everything.
So it showed me the importance of studying.
We were the people of the book, you know?
Very early, everybody studied and studied.
Like I mentioned, my grandfather studied all his life, with the [INAUDIBLE] rabbi [INAUDIBLE].
You know, it was like a [INAUDIBLE], where he had, with a lot of Hasidim.
And my grandmother, great-grandmother, [?
Itka, ?] after whom I'm named, used to, to-- whatever they-- business they had-- had some kind of business, she used to be in the business for make a living.
Yeah, very.
Strictly!
And my grandmother was very-- my grandfather-- I have pictures, I'll show you, of them-- pictures-- beards, everybody, you know?
Most people, you know, in the, in the small shtetls which we lived were, were religious.
We only had one Orthodox shul in our, in our shtetl, to which we belonged.
My father would, every day, whenever he was home-- you know, he was making a living in photography and in picture-framing.
That was-- he had two occupations, during the 13 years of my-- first 13s of my life.
At first, he was in, in wholesale dairy business, with a partner.
For some reason, he switched.
I think it doesn't work out, the partnership or something.
No, it was-- they were, they were making butter, and you know, like-- like in a wholesale place.
So later he switched, and he did photography.
You know?
And so part-- the photography, he used to do in the house.
He was very much involved in raising the children and participating with us.
And the photography, he was [INAUDIBLE].
So I was always had two people at home-- my grandmother, and-- I remember, summer we used to go away.
I never went to a camp, all my life.
Never was away a day from my parents, alone, till age 13.
But every summer-- my mother, as I mentioned, had the-- she was one of seven.
But unfortunately, two died.
The older son and the youngest daughter died.
What were their names?
The oldest, I don't know.
But because-- maybe because he didn't live, so they didn't-- but the youngest, I do know-- [?
Kyla. ?] I have a photograph of her.
In fact, I never saw the photograph before the war.
I was lucky.
I got it after the war.
And I'll tell you.
Yeah.
Before I was born yet.
So, but the others, I had one uncle which at age 18 emigrated from Poland to England, to London, England.
There he met and married a woman who came with a large family from Russia.
Brest-Litsk, ?] I think it was called.
And they got married, and they had six children.
Also was very religious.
And they left--
What was the name?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wrote down the names.
I don't remember.
I know it was Gerald, Bob, [?
Esme-- ?]
Oh, my uncle?
Moishe Pinchas Zebrak.
Zebrak.
That was my grandmother's.
Z-E-B-R-A-K.
They lived in England.
I never met them personally, but they used to write letters and photographs.
And then she had a sister who lived in Poland.
And their name was Lencznowlsky.
Ruchel ?] and Shlomo Lencznowlsky.
L-E-N-C-Z-N-O-W-L-S-K-Y.
Lencznowlsky.
And they had three children-- Shmulek, [?
Basha, ?] and one more-- Sheindel.
And they used to come every summer.
You remember, I started to tell you, every summer we'd go to the country, to the village.
There-- also, I want to mention, whenever we went there, my life was different, from food to everything.
For instance, here, everything-- everybody's aware of watching not to eat too much fat.

There, we used to go twice a day, in the morning and the evening, and to drink milk, fresh from the cow, when they, when they milked the cow, was still-- and I have wonderful memories, walking with my mother, and--
We didn't own it.
We rented, you know?
In [?
Golot, ?] it was called.
It was out-- we were like a little-- not too far from Ciechonw.
We used to go there every summer when I was a child.

And I remember my mother used to pluck flowers and make, like, a little crown on the head, show me which mushrooms were good ones, because it used to-- wooded area, which mushrooms are good, and which are poisonous.
Nuts.
And whenever she went-- she loved nature.
She was, she was-- loving woman.
She loved nature.
She loved people.
She loved life.
And whenever we went there, she would point out, and talk about the seasons, and-- always had conversations.
Always.
She was very-- could express herself well.
And my cousins from Warsaw would come, and the others.
Then, in Ciechonw, we have an aunt and uncle-- Esther [?
Tsiel ?] and Shmuel [?
Yankov ?] Pazdziorek.
Pazdziorek is P-A-Z-D-Z-I-O-R-E-K.
And she would-- she-- they had seven children.
She was Orthodox, like my grandmother.
She was wearing her sheitel-- you know, a wig, but not for the-- to beautify, but is the custom.
And she was very observant.
And they had seven children, six daughters and one son.
And in Poland there was no-- when you-- when a girl had to marry, they had to have a dowry.
So they said, oy, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
To have six daughters, that's not a joke, you know, to marry them off.
But three already were married, you know?
And one-- oh, there is a story that I want to mention.
One of them was called [?
Yitta. ?] You know?
The same name-- named after the same great-grandmother that I was.
But she was-- and she had a boyfriend who was also born in Ciechonw, Poland.
He emigrated with the first Maccabiah.
There was, you know, like-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] he immigrated to Palestine.
When?
She emigrated 1934, he, he a little earlier.
I wrote down, but right now I don't remember.
Anyhow-- and my cousin [?
Yitta ?] went to join him in 1934.
I was eight years old, and I remember everyone from the family was-- we had more relatives which I didn't mention yet.
But the all the relatives came to say goodbye to her.
Everyone was crying and saying, God knows if we will see each other again.
And my grandmother, who was a woman, as I mentioned at the beginning, of deep faith-- had a lot of [YIDDISH]-- faith in God.
And she tried to comfort us.
She said, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]-- children, don't cry.
With God, everything is possible.
In other words, maybe this too for the best.
And she used to say-- you know, like, grew up in a home with different sayings.
She said, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. 'A mountain, a mountain cannot get together, but people can get together.' In other words, don't cry.
Maybe that's for the best.
You know, try to comfort us.
Well, they were, they were Zionist.
They were, belonged to youth organization-- Zionist.
So they-- it wasn't Israel, it was Palestine, then.
And I just--
Well, happy that they were going, because they were going to Palestine.
Sad of the fear whether we will see them again.
And, in fact, my cousin [?
Yitta ?] didn't see any of her family again.
The whole family was gone, just like mine.
And I don't know whether-- should I jump ahead to, to later, with that?
OK, so I'll tell you later a story-- the, the, the continuation of this story, which is very moving, because I always think about my grandmother when I think about this story.
So and then we had an aunt who lived in [INAUDIBLE].
An uncle who lived in [?
Mlawa. ?] His name was [?
Shia. ?] And his-- his wife's name was [?
Dino. ?]
And they had three daughters.
One was also called [?
Yitta, ?] one was Dora, and one Zeisel.
Zebrak.
Because that was my uncle.
So they all lived in Poland.
We had a very big family.
And we were a close family, very close-knit family, because we used to visit each other, you know.
My, my parents used to take me to see them.
We went to see-- they came to us.
My father was born-- my mother was born in Siedlec, Poland.
Siedlec is S-I-E-D-L-E-C.
Siedlec, Poland.
And, as I mentioned, she was one of seven to die.
My father was born in Pultusk, Poland.
I think I mentioned that before.
That's-- and he was one of two siblings.
He had a younger sister, [?
Sure. ?] His parents' name was Zeisel and [?
Srulek ?] Frajman, after-- my brother and sister was named after them.
And my grandfather died.
This is the story my parents used to tell me, about their families.
My grandfather died when my father was a young boy.
And my grandmother remarried, which was very customary in Yiddish-- the religious tried not to stay single, you know.
And she had two children from her second marriage-- [?
Ruchel ?] and [INAUDIBLE].
And they all lived in Pultusk, and they were in dairy business, too.
And I often used to go to Pultusk.
My father would take me.
Not, not very far.
Not very far.
We lived about 90 kilometers north of Warsaw.
This might have been closer.
But to tell you the truth, it's hard for me to estimate, because kilometers are different than, than miles, you know?
But I know it wasn't that long to drive.
Except at that time we didn't drive in cars.
We went by horse and buggy or in a [? dorozka, ?] which was also a horse, but a-- rented.
You know, like, somebody who had a special vehicle, you know, like a wagon that was-- over the head, it had a-- what do you call it?
It's like, shield, just like-- it looked almost like a baby carriage, where you have that-- you know what I mean?
The top?
And-- used to take-- go with a [? dorozka. ?]
There were trains in Poland.
Sometimes, to Warsaw, my father would sometimes go by train and sometimes would go with horse and buggy.
Different, different things.
And we would go to visit.
And I mentioned before, I don't want to repeat myself, that that's how my parents met.
And then my aunt, the only-- my father's sister, you know, natural sister, [?
Sure, ?] was married.
And she had three children.
And they were also named after the great maternal-- paternal grandparents.
Zeisel was the oldest.
There was Chaye, and then was Srulek.
So the two had the same name as my brother and sister.
Because we always used to give name in memory of those who passed away.
They, fortunately-- excuse me, please.
[COUGH] [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
They, fortunately, when Germany came in-- I didn't know the time where they went, but this-- I'm going to come later to it, you know?
But, so we had a very large family, not counting all the second cousins and [NON-ENGLISH], you know, which-- in-laws, and things.
Because my uncle, who lived in Ciechonw, he was one of five brothers.
Five, and I don't know if he had sisters, too.
Because I was a 13-year-old girl.
So I remember those who used to come and visit, mostly, you know, that the family talked about.
So we had a very, very wonderful family.
And, you know, I remember something which really moved me a lot is that, one time, you know, we didn't have big department stores like we have here, and markets.
So on Thursday already my family start preparing for Shabbos-- for the Sabbath meal.
So we used to go to the marketplace.
This is where, on Thursday-- that was where Jewish and Gentiles had the most transactions.
You know, because they used to come.
They would bring the goods-- you know, like livestock and other things from the country that have grown.
The Jewish people would be tailors and, you know, different kinds of tradesmen.
They would trade and sell and buy.
And we used to buy live chickens and, you know, duck and, and geese-- whatever.
And we've-- what was also different than here is that, here, you want a meat that it shouldn't be fat.
There, you used to blow away the fat, as I remember like today-- see if it's fat.
So it should be fat.
And you know, when the mother went to the butcher, to the kosher butcher, give me a fat piece of meat, you know?
And we would buy the, the chickens there and then take them to the ritual slaughterer, which was there.
And one time my mother asked me to go to the ritual slaughter, to [SPEAKING YIDDISH], which means 'to slaughter the chicken.' And I don't know why she said it.
She said, don't tell the chicken that it's going to be killed.
It's [HEBREW], which means-- in Hebrew, it's a sin.
You're not allowed to tell any-- you know, like, you have to-- don't inflict pain on the, on the chicken.
I was thinking, the chicken will understand my language?
You know?
Seemed to me very funny, you know.
And also, in connection with the livestock buying from, from the peasants, is my parents used to-- in Poland, a girl needed a dowry.
So, since you were little, if you had daughters, you started to make the trousseau for the dowry and everything.
So I remember, as today, my mother and grandmother sometimes used to sit, and they plucked the, the chicken, or-- so the chicken feathers they always thew away.
But when it was a geese or a duck, the down, they would, they would save.
And that was later for pillows, for down quilts.

And always she used to say, [NON-ENGLISH], which means 'God willing'-- when Itkele-- I was the oldest, and I was the [? firstborn-- ?] she's going to get married, she's going to have nice, you know, pillows, with down and everything else.
And whenever they were making [INAUDIBLE], that was always their wish, and that's why I feel so horrible that they didn't live to fulfill that wish.
They should be able to take me under the chuppah, which means under the canopy, when I'm going to get married.
Ciechonw was a small town-- as I mentioned, 90 kilometer north of Warsaw.
When I looked out through the window, I saw in the morning little boys with payele, with the Hasidic, you know-- going to the synagogue.
People were religious.
It was-- it had a [NON-ENGLISH], which means a special-- where there were made loans.
Let's say, if somebody was poor and they needed a loan to go in business or to help somebody, people would lend money.
And they would lend them to those.
But, in order not to shame them-- was a prohibition against shaming somebody.
It was not menschlich.
So they would know who they were loaning the money, and the other was-- it was anonymous, you know?
Charity giving was supposed to be anonymous.
Speaking of charity, when I was a little girl, I remember, every morning-- because the story of charity reminds me of something of the grandmother that I was planning to tell you.
Every morning, when I got up, my grandmother would say with me the prayers.
[SPEAKING YIDDISH]-- you know, the prayer.
And every night, before I went to sleep, she would say with me the prayers again-- the, you know, prayers for thanking God for getting up and for to get up-- you know, special prayers.
For everything, there was a prayer.
She would not permit me to take a bite of food without saying, saying a prayer.
And she used to give me coins.
She had a tin can, which she called the 'pushke.' It was for tzedakah, charity box.
And always to put in coins, to teach me tzedakah.
And, and she used to-- always, when she gave me, she gave me coins, she used to say that to help others, those who are less fortunate, is a mitzvah, a commandment.
I remember one time, I was a little girl, still, and a poor man came to our house and asked my grandmother for alms-- for tzedakah.
It was not-- there was no social security or welfare at that time.
And the people used to help the people, you know?
But there were some people who were poor and used to go to beg.
So my grandmother took a few coins, and she gave them to me.
And she said, Itkele, please give it to the man waiting at the door.
At that time, I was still a little girl.
I didn't know one coin from the other.
And I was always a curious girl.
I wanted to know everything, where and why, always asking question.
In fact, my father used to joke with me, Itkele, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
I always want to know where the [? feet ?] are [? growing ?] from.
In other words, to know the beginning, the essence, the mysteries.
So I looked in, in my hand.
And I said, Grandmother!
So much money?
My grandmother smiled, and she said, my child, you only have what you give away.
I couldn't understand.
So I asked my-- so I questioned my grandmother's proverb.
And I said, grandmother, how can you give away something and still have it?
Couldn't understand this, so I questioned her.
So she said, in Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. 'My child, I'm not giving away mine.
I am only giving what God and my parents gave me.' And I often was thinking about this, because everything that God gave us, and my parents gave me, the Nazis took away.
And this is why those stories-- because I see the contrast.
I see the contrast of the values.
And at that time, I didn't question those values.
I just remembered them.
But I'm going to show you, later, some photographs.
And, in connection with the [? sea ?] and with the story of my cousin [?
Yitta. ?] And then, later on, I understood what, what it means to give away.
Because finally, when I became older, I understood that without loving and giving, nothing would be passed on to the next generation.
In fact, this is not what my grandmother told me, but I-- that my mind tells me now.
Words wouldn't be words-- are not words-- before they are spoken.
And deeds are not deeds before they are done.
So nothing could exist without giving.
There is no living without giving.
Nothing would exist.
So I understood the meaning of giving-- giving charity, giving of herself-- the goodness and everything.
Because she was forever giving.
She was not-- as far as I know, she wasn't-- she was a widow, and she was not very rich.
But for Purim, when it came, Purim-- she would always make hamentashen and make little packages and the [SPEAKING YIDDISH]-- you know, would give-- send to this aunt and send to, to the neighbor.
Oh, here is a poor widow.
And sometimes she would cook.
She would cook a little more and send out to them.
So those are the things that I experienced at home, you know?
And, as far as songs, I loved-- since I was child, a child, I loved music, I loved songs, I loved story.
And fortunately, my parents and grandmother were wonderful storytellers.
When we didn't have, you know, like I said, heating-- central heating, or-- like here.
So we had iron cast stove, to cook.
And then we had a tile oven that would be from the floor almost to the ceiling.
And it has, on one side, had an opening.
And for Shabbos, for yuntif, for the holidays, the relatives would come, because my grandmother lived with us.
So everybody came to visit the bubbe.
That was something special.
And they would sit and tell stories, reminiscing about the past, and tell stories about pogroms, and tell stories about weddings, and all different kinds of stories.
And I was a curious girl, so I would sit on the side.
And everybody thought that I don't pay attention.
And I listened and observed everything.
Like a sponge, I was soaking in.
And those stories made a very great impact on my life, you know?
So I remembered all those stories, you know, they were talking about.
But I personally have not experienced-- my only experience with being a prejudiced was that, when I was walking home from school, when the kids beat me up.
That was the only-- plus when they said, you know, the houses are yours, but the streets-- it still rings in my ears.
And one other experience that I had.
But that was not bad.
A bunch-- we were playing, winter, in the snow.
There was a Polish little girl, [?
Halinka. ?] I don't remember any longer her last name.
But I-- the first name I remember, because we used to play sometimes.
My family never discriminated, you know, like, didn't tell me that I couldn't play with somebody.
So we were playing in the snow, and a bunch of kids walked by, and they threw snow at me.
So, and you know, said, again, derogatory things about Jewish.
So she stood up, and she said, she's not Jewish, she's my friend!
And I laughed and cried at the same time.
Because in other words, in order to be my friend, she had to deny that I'm Jewish, which-- she meant well.
She was a very good child.

But, you know, so-- it was the-- because of this here, plus that Orthodox Jews were involved-- most of the time at the synagogue, and involved in, in, in things which-- you know, volunteer charity work, and teaching.
So there was not much contact between the-- we had Polish friends, we had Jewish friends, but there was not that much contact.
